options.text: `string` — the runtime type of `x` is `String`
`object` — overload resolution uses the compile-time type of the argument
It does not compile: the call is ambiguous
It throws a `ClassCastException` at runtime

questions.explanation: Overloading is ad-hoc polymorphism resolved at **compile time**: `x` is declared `Object`, so `log(Object)` is chosen regardless of what `x` holds at runtime. (a) describes *overriding* semantics — dispatch on the runtime receiver — which doesn't apply to arguments. (c) there's no ambiguity; `Object` matches exactly one overload for a variable of static type `Object`. (d) nothing here casts.

questions.prompt: Your codebase has an expression-tree AST (`Literal`, `Add`, `Mul`, …) that gains a **new operation** every sprint (evaluate, pretty-print, optimize, type-check) but almost never a new node type. Which design fits best?

options.text: A sealed node hierarchy processed by exhaustive switches (or visitors) — new operations are added without touching node classes.
An abstract method per operation on the node base class, overridden in every node — the classic OO answer.
A single `Node` class with a `type` string field and a map of properties.
Reflection-based dispatch keyed on class names.

questions.explanation: This is the expression problem: the system grows along the *operations* axis, so switch-on-sealed-type (or Visitor) keeps each operation in one place and adding one touches nothing else. (b) optimizes the wrong axis — every new operation edits every node class. (c) throws away the type system entirely. (d) trades compile-time safety for runtime string matching — the worst of both worlds.

questions.prompt: Which situation is the strongest case for **subtype polymorphism** over a switch on a sealed hierarchy?

options.text: Third parties ship new payment providers as plugins your code must call without being recompiled.
A protocol has exactly four message kinds, fixed by an RFC.
You want the compiler to flag every consumer when a new case is added.
The variants are pure data with no behavior differences.

questions.explanation: An open, externally extensible set of *behaviors* is exactly what virtual dispatch is for: new providers are new classes, and no existing code changes or even knows about them. (b) is a closed set — sealed + exhaustive switch is safer. (c) describes the sealed-switch benefit, not virtual dispatch (which silently 'handles' new subtypes). (d) with no behavior variance there's nothing to dispatch on.

questions.prompt: A teammate calls this method 'fully generic.' What should a reviewer flag?
questions.code:
<T> BigDecimal totalOf(List<T> items) {
    BigDecimal total = BigDecimal.ZERO;
    for (T item : items) {
        if (item instanceof Invoice inv) {
            total = total.add(inv.amount());
        } else if (item instanceof Receipt r) {
            total = total.add(r.value());
        }
    }
    return total;
}

options.text: It should use `stream().reduce()` instead of a loop.
The type parameter is fake: the method inspects concrete types, silently returns 0 for anything else, and the signature hides the real requirement.
`BigDecimal` is the wrong type for money.
`instanceof` pattern matching requires a newer JDK and should be avoided.

questions.explanation: The `<T>` promises uniform treatment of any type, but the body is a disguised type switch — worse, unknown types are silently skipped, so `totalOf(List.of(creditNote))` returns 0 with no error. Honest designs: a bounded type (`<T extends Monetary>` with `amount()`), or an explicit sealed hierarchy. (a) is style, and reduce wouldn't fix the dishonest signature. (c) is backwards — BigDecimal is the conventional choice. (d) pattern matching is fine; the problem is what it's used for.

questions.prompt: Which statements about the expression problem are true? Select all that apply.

options.text: Virtual dispatch makes adding new types cheap and adding new operations expensive.
Switch-on-sealed-type makes adding new operations cheap and adding new types expensive.
The Visitor pattern is one classical OO mechanism for choosing the 'operations grow' side.
Modern languages have solved the expression problem, so the trade-off no longer applies in practice.

questions.explanation: (a) and (b) are the two sides of the trade-off: each approach is 'open' along one axis and 'closed' along the other. (c) Visitor is exactly the OO encoding of operation-centric growth — each visitor is one operation over a fixed node set. (d) is false in practice: research solutions exist (type classes, open multimethods), but in mainstream Java/Kotlin/C#/Dart you still pick an axis, and pretending otherwise leads to designs that fight the language.

questions.prompt: Which is an example of **parametric** polymorphism?

options.text: `Stack<T>` storing and returning elements of any type without inspecting them
`Animal.speak()` overridden by `Dog` and `Cat`
`print(int)` and `print(double)` overloads
A switch statement over an enum

questions.explanation: `Stack<T>` is one implementation that works uniformly for every element type — the definition of parametric polymorphism. (b) is subtype polymorphism (runtime dispatch on the receiver). (c) is ad-hoc polymorphism (compile-time overload selection). (d) is ordinary control flow, not polymorphism at all.
